message:
perf(raptorq): sparse-first column ordering, hybrid elimination, and chunked GF256 scalar kernels

Dense-core Gaussian elimination now reorders columns by ascending sparse
support count before entering the elimination loop. This deterministic
sparse-first ordering reduces expected fill-in and cuts gauss_ops by ~40%
on the hard-regime golden vectors (RQ-D1-E2E-001: 415→247, E2E-003: 400→250).

A hybrid sparse/dense row-update cost model (3/5 threshold) detects when
the pivot row is sparse enough that iterating only its nonzero column
indices is cheaper than the full dense sweep. Both the decode() and
decode_with_trace() paths receive identical changes for consistency.

In GF256, the scalar fallback paths for mul_slice and addmul_slice now
use table-driven 8-element unrolled loops instead of per-element log/exp
lookups. The mul_with_table_scalar and addmul_with_table_scalar functions
are promoted from #[cfg(test)] to production code so short slices below
the SIMD threshold still benefit from table-driven processing. A new
module-level ADDMUL_TABLE_THRESHOLD constant replaces the function-local
one for consistency with MUL_TABLE_THRESHOLD.

The non-SIMD addmul_with_table_wide path is rewritten to use u64
word-width XOR operations (read 8 src bytes → table-lookup → pack into
u64 → XOR with dst word) to cut memory round-trips on platforms without
SIMD intrinsics.

New unit tests cover sparse_first_dense_columns ordering,
should_use_sparse_row_update cost model decisions, and
pivot_nonzero_columns stability.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
